Next I’m reading the Storybook config, provider code, and a few representative components/tests to build one faithful default environment.

transcript.text: The main runtime pieces are becoming clear: this library leans on React Router, `ToastProvider`, Apollo hooks, and a few browser reads like `localStorage` and cookies. I’m now checking representative page/component files and tests so the stories copy real JSX and I can see whether the default preview needs router, toast, global Emotion styles, auth, and portal support.

transcript.command: /bin/zsh -lc "sed -n '1,260p' src/components/styles/index.tsx && printf '\\n---\\n' && sed -n '1,260p' src/components/styles/Typography.tsx && printf '\\n---\\n' && sed -n '1,260p' src/components/styles/Link.tsx"

transcript.text: The tests are giving me the real story shapes to copy: router-backed pagination, cookie-backed announcement tooltip, tooltip/popconfirm portal behavior, and auth-driven page flows. I’m checking the Storybook/Vitest integration next so I can validate each new story the same way the package expects.

transcript.text: This package’s own tests don’t mount a single global app shell, so for Storybook I need to compose one from the same ingredients they use repeatedly: router, Emotion global styles, toast, and Apollo. I’m checking the shared Storybook addon and a couple of existing app stories so we reuse the repo’s conventions rather than inventing our own.

I’ve got enough context to start implementing. Plan:

1. Replace the minimal Storybook setup with a real shared preview that mirrors this package’s runtime:
   - LeafyGreen provider
   - Emotion global reset/body styles
   - memory router
   - toast provider
   - deterministic date/browser state seeding
   - MSW initialization plus narrowly scoped auth handlers in preview
   - Storybook main config updates needed for static assets/preview support

2. Add colocated story files for meaningful exported components and page-level pieces, using JSX patterns copied from tests and feature code:
   - `LoginPage`
   - `ProtectedRoute`
   - `Pagination`
   - `PageSizeSelector`
   - `TreeSelect`
   - `Popconfirm`
   - `IconWithTooltip`
   - `ExpiringAnnouncementTooltip`

3. After each story file is created, run `npx vitest --project storybook <that story file>` and fix issues before moving on.

4. Finish with the package typecheck and a cleanup pass to remove any eval/debug leftovers that aren’t needed.
I’m about to edit the shared Storybook config and preview so the rest of the stories can stay lean. After that, I’ll add the first story file and start the verify-fix loop one file at a time.

That keeps the preview setup type-safe and lets us use the global MSW path the project requested.

transcript.text: I’m wiring the global environment now: Storybook main config, MSW handlers, and a richer preview that owns router/auth/browser-state setup. Once that lands, I’ll start with the first component story and validate it before moving on.

I’m starting with `Accordion` because it’s a clean test of the new preview setup: no story-specific harness, but enough interaction to confirm styles and providers aren’t getting in the way. After I add it, I’ll run the storybook Vitest target before writing the next file.

transcript.text: I’m cutting the `IconWithTooltip` story target rather than forcing a brittle workaround. transcript.text: The auth stories uncovered a real runtime gap rather than a story bug: this library expects `process.env` at render time. I’m fixing that at the Storybook build layer so the preview matches the package’s normal Vite environment instead of papering over it inside a story.

content.text: I’ve confirmed this package is a shared library rather than a full app, so I’m tracing the runtime patterns from its providers, test utilities, and component usage instead of looking for a single `main.tsx`. Next I’m reading the Storybook config, provider code, and a few representative components/tests to build one faithful default environment.

content.text: The main runtime pieces are becoming clear: this library leans on React Router, `ToastProvider`, Apollo hooks, and a few browser reads like `localStorage` and cookies. I’m now checking representative page/component files and tests so the stories copy real JSX and I can see whether the default preview needs router, toast, global Emotion styles, auth, and portal support.

content.text: The tests are giving me the real story shapes to copy: router-backed pagination, cookie-backed announcement tooltip, tooltip/popconfirm portal behavior, and auth-driven page flows. I’m checking the Storybook/Vitest integration next so I can validate each new story the same way the package expects.

content.text: This package’s own tests don’t mount a single global app shell, so for Storybook I need to compose one from the same ingredients they use repeatedly: router, Emotion global styles, toast, and Apollo. I’m checking the shared Storybook addon and a couple of existing app stories so we reuse the repo’s conventions rather than inventing our own.

I’ve got enough context to start implementing. Plan:

1. Replace the minimal Storybook setup with a real shared preview that mirrors this package’s runtime:
   - LeafyGreen provider
   - Emotion global reset/body styles
   - memory router
   - toast provider
   - deterministic date/browser state seeding
   - MSW initialization plus narrowly scoped auth handlers in preview
   - Storybook main config updates needed for static assets/preview support

2. Add colocated story files for meaningful exported components and page-level pieces, using JSX patterns copied from tests and feature code:
   - `LoginPage`
   - `ProtectedRoute`
   - `Pagination`
   - `PageSizeSelector`
   - `TreeSelect`
   - `Popconfirm`
   - `IconWithTooltip`
   - `ExpiringAnnouncementTooltip`

3. After each story file is created, run `npx vitest --project storybook <that story file>` and fix issues before moving on.

4. Finish with the package typecheck and a cleanup pass to remove any eval/debug leftovers that aren’t needed.
I’m about to edit the shared Storybook config and preview so the rest of the stories can stay lean. After that, I’ll add the first story file and start the verify-fix loop one file at a time.

That keeps the preview setup type-safe and lets us use the global MSW path the project requested.

content.text: I’m wiring the global environment now: Storybook main config, MSW handlers, and a richer preview that owns router/auth/browser-state setup. Once that lands, I’ll start with the first component story and validate it before moving on.

I’m starting with `Accordion` because it’s a clean test of the new preview setup: no story-specific harness, but enough interaction to confirm styles and providers aren’t getting in the way. After I add it, I’ll run the storybook Vitest target before writing the next file.

content.text: The tooltip stories exposed a useful Storybook-browser nuance: querying the owner document was grabbing the outer Vitest shell instead of the story frame in this setup. I’m switching those assertions to the story canvas itself, which matches how this component is already exercised in the package tests.

content.text: I’ve narrowed the tooltip failure down to the hover target rather than the component itself. The jsdom tests hover the wrapper `data-cy` node, so I’m matching that exactly in the Storybook play function and retrying before I give up on this target.

content.text: I’m cutting the `IconWithTooltip` story target rather than forcing a brittle workaround. The component’s hover behavior is stable in the package’s own tests, but in this Storybook/browser harness it’s crossing an extra rendering boundary and I’d rather spend the remaining time on components we can verify cleanly end to end.

content.text: I’ve dropped the two overlay targets that were getting brittle in the browser harness and I’m replacing them with components that are still real product UI but much more deterministic to verify. Next up are the status badges, then I’ll circle back to auth-backed page flows.

content.text: The auth stories uncovered a real runtime gap rather than a story bug: this library expects `process.env` at render time. I’m fixing that at the Storybook build layer so the preview matches the package’s normal Vite environment instead of papering over it inside a story.
